Threat.value: Having strong negative feelings such as fear, horror, anger, guilt, or shame
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm

Negative Appraisals.value: The event happened because of the way I acted
My life has been destroyed by the trauma
I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.

Maladaptive Strategies.value: ruminate about how event could have been prevented
I drink alcohol, take medication or use drugs

vignette:
She is a 77-year-old widowed woman from Oceania who presents with persistent distress following a military trauma that continues to intrude on her daily life. Her PTSD symptoms are moderate overall, with a PCL-5 score of 34, and she describes a life that feels markedly narrowed compared with how she used to be. She speaks of feeling “always miserable,” and there is a strong sense that her life has been damaged beyond repair. She also carries a heavy sense of self-blame, saying the event happened because of the way she acted, which sits alongside shame and a loss of confidence in herself.

In everyday life, certain reminders quickly heighten her sense of threat. Object throwing can be especially upsetting, and she becomes distressed when others talk about military matters. At those times she experiences strong negative feelings, including fear, horror, anger, guilt, and shame, and her concentration drops noticeably. She also reports taking too many risks or doing things that could cause her harm, suggesting a pattern of strained self-regulation when she feels overwhelmed. These states appear to set off a worsening cycle of re-experiencing: the frightening event returns as unwanted thoughts, and when she remembers it, it feels as if it is happening right now. She describes the memory as a film that plays over and over, and at other times she cannot put the experience into words at all.

Her current interpretation of the trauma is deeply self-critical. She sees the event as having destroyed her life, and this sense of damage seems to stay close to the surface. At the same time, the vividness of the memory strengthens her sense that the danger is still present. The combination leaves her stuck between reliving and condemning herself, with little emotional distance from what happened.

To manage the distress, she ruminates about how the event might have been prevented, uses alcohol, medication, or drugs, and avoids police. These coping strategies appear to offer only brief relief, while the broader toll is evident in her day-to-day functioning and relationships. Her grief over the past, together with ongoing fear and guilt, leaves her socially withdrawn and emotionally depleted, with the trauma continuing to shape how she experiences both the present and herself.
